steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures being connected
steps.content: The sentence has an independent clause: “NATO's principle of collective defense confers both benefits and obligations.” This must connect to what follows: “the organization's many members… are all bound to defend Canada, the reverse is also true.” The core task is choosing the right punctuation and connector to join them.

steps.title: Match the relationship and apply the rule
steps.content: The passage shows a relationship of concurrence or contrast between the principle's obligations and the members' defensive commitments. A comma before a subordinating conjunction like “while” correctly forms a complex sentence, where the dependent clause modifies the main clause. Choice C uses “comma + while,” creating: “…confers both benefits and obligations, while the organization's many members are bound to defend Canada, the reverse is also true.” This properly attaches the dependent clause. Choice A misuses a colon—colons do not introduce dependent clauses. Choice B uses a semicolon, which joins independent clauses, but the second part contains a comma splice (“Canada, the reverse is also true”), making it incorrect. Choice D creates a new sentence but preserves the same comma splice issue within it. That matches choice C — C is correct.
